coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent sensor based on a magnetic covalent organic framework-supported, carbon dot-embedded molecularly imprinted composite for the specific optosensing of bisphenol A in foods
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haiyang
Jiang, Shanshan
Xu, Zhixiang
zhou, Shuang
Xu, Longhua
coredata.dc:description: An efficient one-pot strategy for the synthesis of a bisphenol A (BPA) fluorescent sensor based on a magnetic covalent organic framework-supported, carbon dot-embedded molecularly imprinted composite (named MCOF@CD@MIP) is reported for the first time in this work. Herein, MCOFs were prepared via the simple in-situ growth of COF crystals on Fe3O4 nanoparticles and acted as supports to provide a large surface area for CD-embedded MIP layer deposition through a room temperature sol-gel molecular imprinting process. The fabricated MCOF@CD@MIP integrated the useful magnetic properties of Fe3O4, the signal transduction function of CDs, the signal amplification effect derived from the COFs and MIP, and the specific recognition behavior of MIP toward template molecule BPA. Based on the fluorescence quenching behavior of BPA for the MCOF@CD@MIP, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent MCOF@CD@MIP sensor was developed over the concentration range of 25–3000 μg L-1 with a low detection limit (12 μg L-1). Furthermore, the MCOF@CD@MIP sensor was employed to detect BPA in canned goods and boxed drinks with satisfactory results, which agreed well with those obtained by the traditional high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method, demonstrating the good reliability, accuracy and practicality of our constructed sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic covalent organic frameworks
Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensing
Molecularly imprinted
Bisphenol A

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and composition analysis of the prepared MCOF@CD@MIP
Characterization of magnetic properties of MCOF@CD@MIP
Characterization of the thermal stability of MCOF@CD@MIP
Characterization of the surface area and pore volume of MCOF@CD@MIP
The FL response behavior of MCOF@CD@MIP to BPA
The FL response selectivity of MCOF@CD@MIP toward BPA
The dynamic FL response of MCOF@CD@MIP for BPA
The repeatability of the MCOF@CD@MIP sensor
Development of the FL sensing detection method for BPA using MCOF@CD@MIP
Quantitative detection of BPA in real samples using the MCOF@CD@MIP sensor
Comparison of the performance of the developed MCOF@CD@MIP sensor with previously reported sensors for BPA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) MCOFs and (B) MCOF@CD@MIP. HAADF-STEM image coupled with elemental distribution maps(C) and EDS mapping(D) of MCOF@CD@MIP.

. (A) FT-IR spectra of MCOFs (a), MCOF@CD@MIP (b) and MCOF@CD@NIP (c). (B) Wide angle XRD patterns of MCOFs (a) and MCOF@CD@MIP (b) (insert: small angle XRD patterns of MCOFs (a) and MCOF@CD@MIP (b) and Fe3O4 (c)). (C) Hysteresis loops of Fe3O4 (a), MCOFs (b) and MCOF@CD@MIP (c). (D) TGA curves of MCOFs (a) and MCOF@CD@MIP (b).

. (A) FL stability of MCOF@CD@MIP.(B) FL response behavior of MCOF@CD@NIP (a) and MCOF@CD@MIP after (b) and before (d) extraction, and (c) MCOF@CD@MIP with the addition of BPA (1.0 mg L-1). The selectivity of the FL response of MCOF@CD@MIP (C) and MCOF@CD@NIP (D) toward BPA, BPAF, BPS, BPB, HQ and BPF. (E) Dynamics of the FL response of MCOF@CD@MIP (a) and MCOF@CD@NIP (b) to BPA with an initial concentration of 1.0 mg L-1 at different times (1 min, 2 min, 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 60 min, 90 min). (F) Cycle test of MCOF@CD@MIP in the absence (upper dot) and presence (lower dot) of BPA (1.0 mg L-1).

FL spectra of MCOF@CD@MIP (A), MCOF@CD@NIP (B), CD@MIP (C) and CD@NIP (D) at different concentrations of BPA solutions, ranging from 0 to 3.0 mg L-1 (insert: linear relationship between BPA concentration and FL response, n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the fabrication of the MCOF@CD@MIP sensor for BPA.
Results of the determination of BPA in samples by the MCOF@CD@MIP sensor and HPLC.

row.entry: BPA contents detected by developed MCOF@CD@MIP FL sensor (μg kg-1, ± SD, n = 3)
BPA contents detected by HPLC method (μg kg-1, ± SD, n = 3)
Canned yellow peach in metal box

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery results of the determination of BPA in samples by the MCOF@CD@MIP sensor and HPLC.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent sensor based on a magnetic covalent organic framework-supported, carbon dot-embedded molecularly imprinted composite for the specific optosensing of bisphenol A in foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province, College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province, College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Food Processing Technology and Quality Control in Shandong Province, College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an, Shandong, 271018, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient one-pot strategy for the synthesis of a bisphenol A (BPA) fluorescent sensor based on a magnetic covalent organic framework-supported, carbon dot-embedded molecularly imprinted composite (named MCOF@CD@MIP) is reported for the first time in this work. Herein, MCOFs were prepared via the simple in-situ growth of COF crystals on Fe3O4 nanoparticles and acted as supports to provide a large surface area for CD-embedded MIP layer deposition through a room temperature sol-gel molecular imprinting process. The fabricated MCOF@CD@MIP integrated the useful magnetic properties of Fe3O4, the signal transduction function of CDs, the signal amplification effect derived from the COFs and MIP, and the specific recognition behavior of MIP toward template molecule BPA. Based on the fluorescence quenching behavior of BPA for the MCOF@CD@MIP, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent MCOF@CD@MIP sensor was developed over the concentration range of 25–3000 μg L-1 with a low detection limit (12 μg L-1). Furthermore, the MCOF@CD@MIP sensor was employed to detect BPA in canned goods and boxed drinks with satisfactory results, which agreed well with those obtained by the traditional high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method, demonstrating the good reliability, accuracy and practicality of our constructed sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Covalent organic frameworks (COFs), as an emerging class of porous organic materials, have attracted considerable attention since their first report [1–3]. Different from metal‐organic frameworks (MOFs), which could be obtained via the coordinative assembly between metal ions and organic ligands as the building units [4], COFs are linked to form through irreversible covalent bonds, which can be formed, broken, and reformed, allowing reaction systems with “error checking” and “proof‐reading” characteristics to result in the formation of the most thermodynamically stable structures [5]. Now, a variety of COFs have been constructed via different synthetic methodologies through mechanochemical, solvothermal, microwave, and room temperature approaches [6–8]. The periodic and predictable structures, tunable pore size and surface area, excellent structural stability, and abundant functional groups make COFs one of the most promising candidates for a broad array of applications in gas storage [9], separation [10,11], catalysis [12,13], optoelectronics devices [14], drug delivery and sensing [15].

Nevertheless, unlike metal organic frameworks (MOFs), the morphology of COFs is generally irregular, and shapeless bulk materials always form due to the difficulties in controlling their growth rationally [16]. In addition, the inherent low density of COFs materials makes it difficult to collect or precipitate from the matrix [17]. These unexpected characteristics bring inconvenience and obstacles to their further use, and as well as lead to their low efficiency as adsorption material. Therefore, exploring novel COFs with various functionalities is of great significance in improving the potential performance of COFs and extending their applicability. Recently, COF-based composites have been developed through the integration of COFs with other guest functional units, such as metal nanoparticles [18], metal oxide nanoparticles [19] and MOFs [20,21].

Among these functional units, magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4), due to their low cost, superior magnetic response, and very large surface-to-mass ratio, which provide plentiful active sites for COF growth, are considered to be an excellent guest material for the construction of COF-based absorption and separation materials [22,23]. To date, several magnetic COFs have been synthesized for the enrichment and separation of target analytes and further used in some analytical technologies [10,24]. For instance, Fe3O4 @TpPa-1magnetic COFs were synthesized via the solvothermal growth of TpPa-1 COFs on Fe3O4 nanoparticles and used for enrichment of N-glycopeptides from human serum digests [25]. Well-defined Fe3O4 @TbBd core-shell MCOFs were prepared through a rapid room temperature route, and employed for selective enrichment of peptides from biological samples by relying on the π-π stacking interaction and interconnected mesoporous channels of COFs [26]. The approach was further applied for the solid-phase extraction of endocrine-disrupting phenols by Deng and coworkers [27]. The controllable preparation of Fe3O4 @TbBd core-shell MCOF nanospheres was implemented via a monomer-mediated in situ growth strategy by Yan and his coworkers, and the Fe3O4 @TbBd was applied for the adsorption and removal of bisphenol AF and BPA through π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding [17]. COF/TpPa-1 MCOFs with a bouquet-like structure were also synthesized by grafting a COF onto the surface-modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles for the extraction of organic targets [28]. With further development, Chen et al. synthesized Fe3O4 @TbBd as an adsorbent for magnetic solid-phase extraction (MSPE), combined with HPLC-MS, to develop a trace estrogen detection method in human urine samples. Shi et al. employed the constructed Fe3O4 @TpDA as a MSPE sorbent for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons prior to HPLC-DAD analysis, and realized their trace detection in edible oils and grilled meats samples [29].

Due to the lack of signal transduction functions, the chemical sensing applications of MCOFs have not yet been well explored. Because fluorescent (FL) sensors are a promising analytical method with the advantages of low sample requirements, low cost, simple operation, short analysis time and high sensitivity [30], carbon dots (CDs), with their unique properties such as highly luminescent stability, low biological toxicity, and easy access [31,32], have been used to fabricate a variety of fluorescent probes and sensors. This motivated us to introduce highly luminescent CDs into MCOFs to construct a highly sensitive fluorescent sensor.

Indeed, in our previous work [33], we described the construction of FL sensors based on MOFs and CDs through an efficient molecular imprinting process. Molecular imprinting, especially sol-gel molecular imprinting, has been proven to be an effective technology that not only allows the incorporation of CDs into the network followed by attachment onto the surface of the host materials but more importantly generates an optically transparent silicon-based molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) with predetermined selective recognition sites for template molecules while ensuring the fluorescence characteristics of CDs [34,35].

In this work, a novel highly selective and sensitive FL sensor based on an MCOF-supported, CD-embedded MIP optosensing material (MCOF@CD@MIP) was designed and constructed for the first time. Herein, the MCOF in the center provides a feasible magnetic field for the FL sensor and acts as a support to provide a large surface area for surface imprinting strategies, in which the binding sites are more readily accessible for target molecular at the surface of the imprinted matrix rather than the inside for the bulk polymerization. The introduced MIP shell further endows this absorption behavior with high selectivity, and the incorporated CDs can further transduce these chemical events to detectable FL signals. As a proof of concept, BPA, one of the most common endocrine-disrupting chemicals, was selected as a target (template) molecule. BPA has attracted increasing attention worldwide due to its hazard to human health and its wide distribution in environmental and food matrices as well as food packing materials. The constructed MCOF@CD@MIP sensor with signal transduction and signal amplification was successfully applied for selectively capturing and sensitively sensing BPA in canned goods and boxed drinks with satisfactory performance.

MCOFs were synthesized via the in situ growth of highly crystalline imine-linked COF crystals on the Fe3O4 surface using 1,1,2,2-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl)ethane (4PE) and terephthalaldehyde (TPA) as organic ligands according to the reported reference [36,37]. Organosilane-functionalized CDs were prepared via a one-step reaction in accordance with the reference [38] and were employed as a fluorescent signal material. MCOF@CD@MIP was synthesized in a one-pot reaction as described in Scheme 1 under the optimized conditions: template molecular BPA (1 mmol), functional monomer APTES (3 mmol) and fluorescent CDs (500 μL, 0.025 mg L−1) were mixed in ethanol (20 mL) under mechanical stirring for 3 h to prepare the preassembly solution. Then, MCOFs (100 mg) were added, and stirring was maintained for another 1 h. After that, the crosslinkers TEOS (8 mmol) and NH3·H2O (1 mol L-1, 1 mL) were added dropwise to initiate the copolymerization reaction at room temperature in 0.5 h. The resultant MCOF@CD@MIP composite was obtained after aging with heat treatment at 60 °C for 12 h, washing with a mixture of acetic acid and methanol (1:9, v/v) until no BPA could be detected by HPLC. It was then collected by magnetic separation and dried at 60 °C under vacuum.

For comparison, the corresponding non-imprinted polymer-MCOF@CD@NIP and a conventional bulk polymer-CD@MIP were also prepared by following the above conditions excepted the absence of BPA and MCOFs, respectively.

All the FL intensity measurements were carried out under the same conditions: the slit widths were both 10 nm for excitation and emission, the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, the voltage of photomultiplier tube was set to 600 V, and the recording.

emission range was 400–600 nm. The synthesized MCOF@CD@MIP, MCOF@CD@NIP and CD@MIP powders were dispersed thoroughly in methanol or BPA methanol solution with a given suspension density of 1 mg mL-1 to record their FL intensity. In this study, the FL response degree was indicated by the ratio of the initial FL intensity (F0) and the FL intensity after reaction (F).

The fluorescent MCOF@CD@MIP nanoparticles were synthesized as illustrated in Fig. 1. First, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared and used as a magnetic core to grow COF crystals for MCOF synthesis, with the polymerlization of the organic ligands 4PE and TPA. Using MCOFs as the support carrier and CDs as the fluorescent signal material, the final MCOF@CD@MIP nanoparticles were prepared via an efficient one-pot sol-gel molecular imprinting process. When accompanied by the hydrolysis and crosslinking of the alkoxysilane groups in CDs, APTES and TEOS, fluorescent CDs were incorporated into the Si-O-Si framework network, and the CD@MIP film was deposited on the MCOF surface. After the removal of BPA, the final MCOF@CD@MIP composite was obtained. In order to make MCOF@CD@MIP have the best performance for sensing BPA, the critical synthetic parameters including the amounts of CDs and MCOF, the ratio of APTES and TEOS, and the catalyst type, were investigated. As shown in Table.S1-S4, using the imprinted factors (IF, IF=Ksv, MCOF@CD@MIP/ Ksv, MCOF@CD@NIP) as the assessment index, the optimized synthetic conditions were determined to be 500 μL of CDs, 100 mg of MCOFs, and the molar ratio of 1:3:8 for BPA: APTES: TEOS with ammonia catalyzing.
A series of characterizations was conducted, as shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2 to verify the successful synthesis of MCOF@CD@MIP and investigate its physical and chemical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition analysis of the prepared MCOF@CD@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: As expected, the addition of Fe3O4 nanoparticles improve the irregular and shapeless bulk morphology of pure COFs, and make the MCOF and MCOF@CD@MIP have a typical core-shell structure in TEM images. Fig. 1A shows that the MCOFs had spherical morphology with an approximate size of 300 nm, and the thickness of the COF shell was approximately 30 nm. Fig. 1B shows that compared to the COF shell, a much rougher shell surrounded the MCOFs, resulting from the deposition of the CD@MIP layer on the MCOFs, which indicated the successful construction of core-shell structure MCOF@CD@MIP. These assignments were further confirmed by elemental mapping (Fig. 1C) and EDS images (Fig. 1D), which revealed the elemental distribution and composition of N, O, Fe, Si and C in MCOF@CD@MIP.

FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2A a~c) were employed to probe the functional groups in MCOFs, MCOF@CD@MIP and MCOF@CD@NIP and verify their successful preparation. Except for the vibrations of the benzene skeleton at 1450–1600 cm-1, as shown in Fig. 2Aa, the characteristic peak at 1623 cm−1 belonging to the CN vibration generated from the condensation reaction of 4PE and TPA, and the obvious absorption peak at 569 cm-1 was corresponded to Fe-O-Fe vibration, validating the formation and coating of COF shells for Fe3O4. For MCOF@CD@MIP (b) and MCOF@CD@NIP (c), the peaks located at 1058 cm-1 and 781 cm-1 were ascribed to the Si-O-Si stretching and Si-O vibrations, respectively, which indicated the formation of the Si-O-Si framework. Compared to the MCOFs (a), the new absorption peaks at 1656 cm-1 and 1552 cm-1 were attributed to the amide I and II vibrations of CDs, proving the incorporation of CDs into the polymer networks. In addition, the appearance of similar peaks in MCOF@CD@MIP (b) and MCOF@CD@NIP (c) indicated the efficient removal of template molecular BPA. All these data suggested the successful fabrication of MCOF@CD@MIP nanospheres.

The crystalline structure of MCOFs (a) and MCOF@CD@MIP (b) nanoparticles were identified by XRD analysis. As displayed in Fig. 2B, the wide-angle XRD of all tested materials exhibited obvious diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 and well corresponds to the previous report [24], indicating their crystallinity was maintained even after coating the MIP shells. In small-angle XRD (the inset of Fig. 2B), compared to the bare Fe3O4 (c), the sharp diffraction peak disappeared in MCOFs (a) and MCOF@CD@MIP (b), which may reflect the crystallinity of the COF shell [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of magnetic properties of MCOF@CD@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The magnetic properties of Fe3O4, MCOFs and MCOF@CD@MIP nanospheres were measured by VSM, as shown in Fig. 2C. The magnetic hysteresis loops displayed a typical paramagnetic characteristic with successively decreasing saturation magnetization values of 63.0 emu g-1, 38.4 emu g-1 and 12.3 emu g-1 for Fe3O4, MCOFs and MCOF@CD@MIP, respectively, due to the increasing coating layers of COF crystals and MIP. This paramagnetic characteristic endowed the MCOF@CD@MIP with a smart response to the externally applied magnetic field, and it only took about 15 s for MCOF@CD@MIP to be separated from the sample extract to realize its recycling and reuse.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the thermal stability of MCOF@CD@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The thermogravimetric (TG) curves in Fig. 2D revealed the thermal stability of MCOF@CD@MIP and MCOFs. When heated from 25° to 300°C, MCOF@CD@MIP and MCOFs exhibited a distinct weight loss of 11%, ascribed to the evaporation of residual solvent (water) and the pyrolysis of silicon hydroxyl groups on the surface of MCOFs and MCOF@CD@MIP. Subsequently, a steep weight loss of 30% was observed in MCOFs, caused by the decomposition of the COF skeletal structure as the temperature was gradually increased to 570 °C, implying a high yield of the COF shells on the surface of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles. In contrast, for MCOF@CD@MIP, only a 16.5% weight loss appeared in the range of 300–1000 °C, which is much less than that for MCOFs because of protection by the MIP layer. It is noteworthy that a long plateau appeared at temperatures under 300 °C, implying the favorable thermal stability of the synthesized MCOF@CD@MIP and further confirming the successful polymerization of MIP on the MCOF surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the surface area and pore volume of MCOF@CD@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The sufficient surface area and appropriate pore volume is very requisite to provide effective active sites, which was conducive to mass transfer and separation. The porous structure of MCOFs (A) and MCOF@CD@MIP (B) was confirmed by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. As displayed in Fig. S1, the type IV isotherms of MCOFs (A) and MCOF@CD@MIP (B) indicates their mesoporous structure. Besides, for MCOFs and MCOF@CD@MIP, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of 187.45 m2 g-1 and 11.89 m2 g-1, and the pore volumes of 0.44 cm3 g-1 and 0.04 cm3 g-1, and the average pore diameters of 24.9 nm and 24.6 nm, were given out respectively. And the values for reported COFs was 2070 m2 g-1, 1.53 cm3 g-1 and 3.8 nm, and these changes were caused by the addition of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and MIP shell. This phenomenon also appeared in others reports [39–41]. Moreover, the Fe3O4 and MIP does not cause the significant changes in the framework structure of the COFs, but also endow MCOF@CD@MIP with permanent magnetism as well as provide accessible sites for template recognition and binding.

Because excellent FL stability is a necessary precondition for the use of MCOF@CD@MIP in practical detection, the FL intensity of an MCOF@CD@MIP suspension (1 mg mL-1) was continuously monitored for 24 h. The results are shown in Fig. 3A. MCOF@CD@MIP showed almost no FL intensity change even when exposed to air, giving a relative standard deviation (RSD) of only 0.5%, which demonstrated its desirable FL stability against photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FL response behavior of MCOF@CD@MIP to BPA
ce:section.ce:para: The FL response behavior of MCOF@CD@MIP and MCOF@CD@NIP to BPA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, both imprinted and nonimprinted materials inherited the FL traits of CDs and maintained a blue FL emission with the maximum peak located at 450 nm. Compared to MCOF@CD@MIP with template molecular BPA (a, 2126), after the removal of BPA, the FL intensity of MCOF@CD@MIP (b, 3395) rose dramatically and recovered to 97.6% of that of MCOF@CD@NIP (c). Importantly, the FL emission intensity of MCOF@CD@MIP (d) was significantly quenched once in the presence of BPA (c) without a shift in the emission peak. The UV–vis absorption and FL excitation and emission peaks are recorded in Fig. S2 to elucidate the mechanism by which BPA quenches MCOF@CD@MIP. Since there was no overlap between the UV–vis absorption of BPA (a) and the FL excitation (c) and emission (d) of MCOF@CD@MIP, energy transfer cannot be the mechanism responsible for this quenching. In addition, the band gap of MCOF@CD@MIP (b) was close to the UV–vis adsorption band of BPA, which made it easy for charges in MCOF@CD@MIP to be transferred to BPA, and thus charge transfer between the CDs (electron donor) and BPA (electron acceptor) was considered to be the possible mechanism of FL quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FL response selectivity of MCOF@CD@MIP toward BPA
ce:section.ce:para: The FL response selectivity of MCOF@CD@MIP toward BPA was evaluated at different incubation concentrations by selecting bis(4-hydroxyphenyl) methylethylmethane (BPB), 4,4-dihydroxydiphenylmethane (BPF), hexafluorobisphenol A (BPAF), 4,4'-sulfonyldiphenol (BPS) with similar size and arrangement of phenol groups, and hydroquinone (HQ) having smaller size and aromatic ring (in Fig. S3) as the structural analogs of BPA. The FL response (F0/F) of MCOF@CD@MIP (in Fig. 3 C) for template BPA was much greater than that of the other analogs, and compared to that of MCOF@CD@NIP (in Fig. 3D), this gap increased more obviously with increasing concentration. In Fig. S4, a maximum imprinted factor of 2.72 was obtained for BPA, which was 2.4 times the next closest BPB, due to the presence of predetermined specific recognition sites, revealing the effectiveness of imprinting technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The dynamic FL response of MCOF@CD@MIP for BPA
ce:section.ce:para: The dynamic FL response of the synthesized MCOF@CD@MIP was determined in a 1.0 mg mL-1 BPA solution with different incubation times, as shown in Fig. 3E. At the beginning of 5 min, the FL response intensity sharply increased and then rapidly reached equilibrium within 10 min. This good mass transfer rate benefited from the introduction of MCOFs that provided large surface support for MIP cavities, which made them more easily accessible for BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The repeatability of the MCOF@CD@MIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The regeneration capacity of the MCOF@CD@MIP was also investigated by the repeated adsorption-desorption of BPA (1.0 mg L-1). The results in Fig. 3F show that within five consecutive cycles, the FL response intensity of MCOF@CD@MIP toward BPA has no significant reduction and always remained above 1.24, and meanwhile the FL intensity of MCOF@CD@MIP could be almost fully (98.7%) recovered to the initial intensity (3463), after regenerating treatment with methanol solution containing 10% acetic acid (3 mL) under ultrasound, revealing that MCOF@CD@MIP will be an ideal renewable FL sensing material for BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of the FL sensing detection method for BPA using MCOF@CD@MIP
ce:section.ce:para:
A fluorescence quenching titration experiment to reveal the dose-dependent interaction between the FL response (F0/F) of the synthesized materials and BPA concentration was conducted by incubating the probes in various initial concentrations of BPA methanol solution. As illustrated in Fig. 4, the FL intensity of MCOF@CD@MIP and MCOF@CD@NIP both decreased with increasing BPA concentration. When the concentration ranged from 25 μg L-1 to 3000 μg L-1, good linear relationships of F
0
/F= 0.204 C
BPA
+ 1.043 (R2 = 0.9986) for MCOF@CD@MIP and F
0
/F= 0.075 C
BPA
+ 1.034 (R2 = 0.983) for MCOF@CD@NIP were revealed by the calibration plots depicted in the insets of Figs. 4A and 4B. Their quenching constants (0.204, 0.075) and IF (2.72) were superior to those obtained from CD@MIP, F
0
/F= 0.056 C
BPA + 1.026 (R2 = 0.999) (in Fig. 4C), and from CD@NIP, F
0
/F= 0.028 C
BPA
+ 1.035 (R2 = 0.981) (in Fig. 4D), indicating the positive effect of the introduction of MCOFs on FL response signal enhancement and amplification as well as the improvement in specific selection ability. Finally, the limit of detection of the developed MCOF@CD@MIP sensor was calculated to be 12 μg L-1 based on 3 times the signal-to-noise ratio, which can realize the highly sensitive and selective sensing analysis of BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of BPA in real samples using the MCOF@CD@MIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility and practicability of the proposed sensing method in real sample analysis, it was applied to the determination of BPA in seven kinds of food products, including canned fruits and luncheon meat as well as bottled fruit juices. The sample preparation is described in the Supporting information. The traditional HPLC method was employed to verify the validity of the analysis results. As shown in Table 1, all the tested samples were detected to contain BPA with contents varying from 5.8 μg kg-1 to 29.6 μg kg-1, which agreed well with the results obtained by the HPLC method. Furthermore, the recovery experiment was also carried out for the canned yellow peach in glasses, luncheon meat in metal cans and peach juice in plastic bottles by the addition of defined amounts of BPA standard solution. The analytical results are listed in Table 2. BPA recoveries ranging from 86.9% to 102.0% were obtained at three different spiking concentrations, which was consistent with the results obtained by the HPLC method, demonstrating good accuracy and stability for sensing trace BPA in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the performance of the developed MCOF@CD@MIP sensor with previously reported sensors for BPA
ce:section.ce:para: Based on different fluorophores and the recognition units such as aptamer and MIP, some FL sensors for BPA were developed, and their performance was listed in Table S5. First, similar to the established FL sensors, our constructed MCOF@CD@MIP sensor with the signal transducing unit, could directly sense the presence or concentration of BPA without the separation process such as chromatography or electrophoresis, and thus largely shortened the analysis time. Secondly, compared to the previously reported FL sensors, our proposed MCOF@CD@MIP sensor exhibited the comparable liner range, acceptable recovery rate, the lower LOD than that based on pyrene/dimethyl-cyclodextrin [42] and MIPs coated gold nanoclusters [30], as well as the expanded application scope from existing water to organic media, which is of great significant for analysis of BPA in complexed sample matrix that usually need to be extracted with organic solvent, although sometimes at the expense of losing some affordable sensitivity compared to the aptamer-based sensor [43,44]. Moreover, to our best knowledge, it is the first time that the magnetic covalent organic framework was introduced to construct the composite probe to prove its signal enhancement and amplification function, and meanwhile realized its fast recycling and reuse.

An efficient and versatile strategy has been presented to construct FL molecularly imprinted sensors based on MCOF nanoparticles for the selective extraction and recognition of BPA. The novel fabricated MCOF@CD@MIP integrated the excellent fluorescence performance of CDs, the separation advantage of magnetic nanoparticles, and the high selectivity of molecularly imprinted composites. MCOF@CD@MIP was successfully employed for sensitively and selectively sensing BPA in complex food matrices. This work provided a simple, economical, and reliable methodology to construct an FL sensing platform by designing renewable magnetic COF@MIP composites for the analysis of BPA and other small-molecule chemical contaminants in food or the environment.

Haiyang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal Analysis, Writing-Original Draft; Shanshan Jiang: Data Curation, Validation. Zhixiang Xu: Conceptualization, Supervision Shuang Zhou: Project Administration, Visualization Longhua Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding Acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing -Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31701684), the Incubation Program for Youth Innovation Team in Shandong Province, the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. ZR2017BC014), and the Training Program for Distinguished Young Talents in Shandong Agricultural University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131729.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Longhua Xu is Associate Professor of Food Science at Shandong Agricultural University. She has a Ph. D. in Food Science from the Tianjin University of Science and Technology, and her research interests are in designing and constructing of novel sensing materials, and her work has been published in journals such as Journal of Materials Chemistry A, Biosensors & Bioelectronics and Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical.

Zhixiang Xu is Professor of Food Science at Shandong Agricultural University. He has a Ph. D. in Food Science from the Tianjin University of Science and Technology, and his research interests are in the integration of molecularly imprinted ploymer and other functional composites for food safety detection.
Haiyang Wang is a Ph. D. candidate in Food Science, and does well in fluorescent material synthesis and application.
Shanshan Jiang got her Master's Degree in Food Processing and Safety, and does well in instrumental analysis of environmental contaminants in food.

Shuang Zhou is Lecturer of Chemical Engineering at Shandong Agricultural University. She has a Ph. D. in Industrial Catalysis from the Tianjin University, and she is good at synthesis and characterization of nanomaterials.
